text.title: Accessibility Evaluation Five-Stage Process
text.summary: Structured five-stage audit process for WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA: automated scan, triage, keyboard testing, screen reader verification, and structured report — with tool pairings and issue priority tiers.

rule.statement: Run automated scanning (axe-core, Pa11y, Lighthouse) as stage 1, but never claim compliance based on automated results alone — tools catch 30-40% of issues.
rule.rationale: Deque and WebAIM data (2024) confirm automated tools miss the majority of issues: ARIA misuse, focus order in modals, live-region behavior, cognitive accessibility, and text-over-image contrast are invisible to headless scanners.

rule.statement: Pin the axe-core version per repo; rule additions in minor versions create phantom regressions that derail review cycles.
rule.rationale: axe-core releases new rules in minor versions. An "axe-clean" baseline at v4.8 may produce violations at v4.9 for the same unmodified code, causing false regression alerts.

rule.statement: Test all of: Tab navigation reaching every interactive element, visible focus at all times, logical tab order matching visual flow, skip links, no keyboard traps, and documented shortcuts not conflicting with AT shortcuts.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1, 2.4.3, 2.4.7, and 2.4.11 cover these criteria. Each requires separate verification; automated tools cover none of them reliably.

rule.statement: For SPA frameworks, audit after route transitions and after async state changes; focus management at route change is the #1 SPA accessibility failure.
rule.rationale: Single-page apps frequently drop focus to the document body after navigation, forcing screen reader users to start re-reading from the top of the page.

rule.statement: Use established AT pairings for screen reader testing: NVDA + Firefox, JAWS + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari on Mac, VoiceOver + Safari on iOS, TalkBack + Chrome on Android.
rule.rationale: AT behavior differs by browser pairing. A finding present in NVDA+Firefox may not reproduce in JAWS+Chrome — and vice versa. Pairings are the industry-standard evidence baseline for VPAT/ACR documentation.

rule.statement: Never accept a screen reader finding from an agent that does not include an audio recording or verbatim speech transcript as evidence; fabricated AT transcripts are undetectable and create false compliance records.
rule.rationale: LLMs cannot reproduce NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver speech buffers. Agent-invented transcripts appear plausible but are not evidence of conformance.

section.title: Issue Priority Tiers

rule.statement: Classify every finding into Critical (blocks access completely, fix immediately), High (major barrier with difficult workaround, this sprint), Medium (creates difficulty, next sprint), or Low (enhancement, backlog).
rule.rationale: Consistent priority classification lets stakeholders make resource decisions and produces comparable metrics across audit cycles. Unclassified findings stall remediation planning.

example.note: Missing form label (SC 1.3.1) → Critical: screen reader users cannot identify the field; no workaround. Missing skip link (SC 2.4.1) → High: keyboard users must tab through full navigation on every page.

section.reference: Accessibility audit report template with POUR findings tables and per-issue structure
Playwright + axe-core page scanner emitting per-page JSON for triage agent
